title: Health Management & Humane Harvest
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Book] Butchering Poultry, Rabbit, Lamb, Goat, and Pork
required.author: Adam Danforth

suggested.title: [Book] The Veterinary Book for Sheep Farmers
suggested.author: David C. Henderson

supplementary.title: [Reference] Your local extension livestock health publications

competence_markers.description: The student conducts systematic daily health assessments and maintains health records
The student can recognize and respond to common health conditions in the species they manage
The student can administer subcutaneous injections, trim hooves (if applicable), and perform FAMACHA scoring (if applicable)
The student has participated in humane slaughter and complete carcass processing with demonstrated competence
The student maintains a stocked animal health kit and can explain the use of each item
The student practices biosecurity and can articulate why prevention is more effective than treatment
The student knows when a condition exceeds their competence and requires a veterinarian

content.why_this_matters:
You can tell when an animal is healthy and when something is wrong. Now you learn what to do about it. Practical health management — the ability to assess an animal systematically, treat common conditions, administer medications, and know when you need professional help — is the difference between an animal keeper who can only call the vet and one who handles the 90% of health management that does not require a veterinarian.

This packet also completes the promise you made when you took responsibility for animals: you learn to participate in humane slaughter and processing. If you raise animals for food, you must understand and eventually conduct every step of the process — from the living animal to the food on the table. This is not brutal exposure. It is honest participation in the full food cycle. A person who eats meat but has never faced the reality of how it is produced has outsourced both the labor and the moral weight of their food choices.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Systematic health monitoring** — daily and weekly assessment protocols for each species
2. **Common condition recognition and treatment** — respiratory illness, digestive issues, parasites, injuries
3. **Basic veterinary procedures** — injections, wound care, hoof trimming, FAMACHA scoring
4. **Biosecurity practices** — quarantine, sanitation, disease prevention
5. **Humane slaughter competence** — methods, execution, and the moral framework of raising animals for food
6. **Carcass processing** — from slaughter to packaged meat with food safety throughout
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Systematic Health Monitoring

Health management begins with a system. Random observation catches obvious problems. Systematic monitoring catches early problems — and early problems are treatable problems.

**Daily health check (every animal, every day):**
- **Posture and movement:** Is the animal standing, moving, and bearing weight normally? Lameness, reluctance to move, or unusual posture are early signs of many conditions.
- **Appetite:** Is the animal eating and drinking normally? Reduced appetite is the most common early indicator of illness across all species.
- **Eyes and nose:** Clear, bright eyes with no discharge. Clean nostrils with no mucus or bubbling. Discharge from eyes or nose indicates respiratory or systemic illness.
- **Droppings:** Normal droppings for the species. Diarrhea, blood in stool, or abnormal color or consistency signal digestive problems or parasites.
- **Behavior:** Is the animal behaving normally for its species and individual personality? Separation from the group, excessive vocalization, or lethargy are warning signs.

**Weekly deeper assessment (breeding stock and valuable animals):**
- Body condition scoring: Is the animal maintaining appropriate weight? Can you feel ribs without pressing hard (good condition) or are ribs prominent (thin) or buried under fat (obese)?
- Skin and coat/feather condition: Parasites, hair loss, feather loss outside of molt, wounds, or swelling
- Feet and legs: Hoof condition (goats and sheep need regular hoof assessment), leg soundness, joint swelling

**Record every observation.** A single day's notes may seem insignificant. Three months of records reveal trends that daily memory cannot hold.

### Part 2: Common Conditions by Species

**Respiratory illness (all species):**
Signs: Coughing, sneezing, nasal discharge, labored breathing, wheezing
Causes: Viral, bacterial, or environmental (poor ventilation — the most common cause in poultry)
Response: Isolate affected animals. Improve ventilation. Provide supportive care (electrolytes, warmth). If symptoms worsen or spread rapidly through the flock, call a veterinarian — respiratory outbreaks can devastate a flock in days.

**Internal parasites (all ruminants, especially sheep and goats):**
Signs: Weight loss despite adequate feed, rough coat, diarrhea, anemia (pale mucous membranes), bottle jaw (fluid swelling under the chin)
Assessment: FAMACHA scoring — pull down the lower eyelid and compare the color of the mucous membrane to a standardized chart. Bright red = low parasite load. White or pale pink = heavy load requiring treatment. Fecal egg counts (collecting a fecal sample and examining it under a microscope or sending it to a lab) provide more precise assessment.
Management: Rotational grazing is the primary parasite management tool. Parasites complete their life cycle on pasture — larvae crawl up grass blades and are ingested by grazing animals. Long rest periods between grazing events reduce larval loads. Chemical deworming is a supplement to pasture management, not a substitute. Over-reliance on dewormers breeds drug-resistant parasites — one of the most serious problems in small ruminant production worldwide.

**External parasites (all species):**
Signs: Excessive scratching, feather/hair loss in patches, visible lice or mites (part feathers or fur and examine skin), scabbing
Treatment: Species-appropriate dust baths (poultry — diatomaceous earth mixed with wood ash), topical treatments (permethrin for severe infestations), cleaning and treating housing

**Digestive issues:**
- **Bloat (ruminants):** Distended left side (rumen), labored breathing, discomfort. Emergency condition — can kill in hours. Caused by rapid gas buildup from lush legume pasture or grain overload. Treatment: pass a stomach tube to release gas, or in extreme emergency, trocar (puncture the rumen through the flank to release gas — veterinary procedure but knowledge of it can save an animal's life before the vet arrives).
- **Crop impaction (poultry):** Firm, distended crop that does not empty overnight. Caused by ingestion of long grass, string, or other indigestible material. Treatment: gentle massage, administration of vegetable oil, and withholding feed until the crop empties. If impaction does not resolve within 24 hours, veterinary intervention may be needed.

**Injuries:**
- Clean wounds with dilute chlorhexidine or Betadine solution
- Control bleeding with direct pressure
- Close minor wounds with wound closure strips or veterinary staples (for deeper lacerations)
- Apply antibiotic ointment to prevent infection
- Monitor daily for signs of infection (swelling, heat, discharge, odor)
- Deep puncture wounds, wounds near joints, and wounds that expose bone or tendons require veterinary care

### Part 3: Basic Veterinary Procedures

**Subcutaneous injection (the most common route):**
1. Draw the correct dose into a syringe using an appropriately sized needle
2. Tent the skin (lift a fold of loose skin — the neck or the area behind the shoulder is typical for most species)
3. Insert the needle at an angle under the skin into the space between skin and muscle
4. Aspirate (pull back on the plunger slightly — if blood enters the syringe, you are in a blood vessel; withdraw and try a new spot)
5. Inject the medication slowly
6. Withdraw the needle and apply light pressure to the injection site

This sounds intimidating. It is not difficult once you have practiced. Your first injections should be supervised by your mentor or a veterinarian.

**Hoof trimming (goats and sheep):**
Overgrown hooves cause lameness, discomfort, and foot rot. Regular trimming — every 6-8 weeks — prevents these problems.
1. Restrain the animal (goats can be stood on a milking stand; sheep are tipped onto their rump)
2. Clean the hoof with a brush or pick
3. Trim the overgrown hoof wall with sharp hoof trimmers, following the contour of the sole
4. The goal is a flat, level bottom surface with no overgrown material folding over
5. Avoid cutting into the quick (the pink tissue inside the hoof wall) — if you see pink, stop. If you cause bleeding, apply blood stop powder

**FAMACHA scoring (sheep and goats):**
Pull down the lower eyelid. Compare the color of the inner eyelid to the FAMACHA card:
- Score 1 (deep red): No treatment needed
- Score 2 (red-pink): No treatment needed
- Score 3 (pink): Monitor closely; consider treatment based on other signs
- Score 4 (pink-white): Treat with dewormer
- Score 5 (white): Treat immediately; animal is severely anemic and may need supportive care

### Part 4: Biosecurity

Disease prevention is cheaper, more effective, and less stressful than disease treatment.

**Quarantine:** Every new animal entering your property should be isolated from existing animals for a minimum of 14 days (30 days preferred). This allows time for any illness carried by the new animal to manifest before it can spread to your established flock or herd. Quarantine space should be completely separate — different airspace if possible, at minimum no fence-line contact.

**Sanitation:** Clean housing regularly. Replace bedding when it becomes excessively soiled. Disinfect waterers and feeders periodically. Clean processing equipment thoroughly after every use.

**Visitor management:** People who have been on other farms can carry disease on their boots and clothing. Require boot cleaning or provide disposable boot covers for visitors. This is not paranoia — diseases like avian influenza, Marek's disease, and foot rot spread readily on contaminated footwear.

### Part 5: Humane Slaughter and Processing

This section is not optional. If you raise animals for food, you must understand and participate in every step of the process.

**The moral framework:**
You took responsibility for this animal's life. You fed it, sheltered it, and kept it healthy. Now you take responsibility for its death. This is not a contradiction — it is the completion of the commitment. The animal lived well because of your care. It dies quickly and without suffering because of your skill. Every person who eats meat is implicated in this process. The difference is that you face it directly rather than outsourcing it.

**Poultry slaughter:**
1. **Catch and calm the bird.** Handle confidently. A stressed bird produces tougher meat and the process is harder for everyone.
2. **Restrain.** A kill cone (an inverted cone that holds the bird upside down with its head protruding through the bottom) is the safest and most effective restraint.
3. **Kill.** Sever the jugular veins and carotid arteries with a single, firm, precise cut on each side of the neck using a very sharp knife. The bird bleeds out rapidly and loses consciousness within seconds. Alternatively, cervical dislocation (breaking the neck) produces instantaneous loss of consciousness.
4. **Bleed out.** Allow 1-2 minutes for complete bleeding. The body will convulse — this is a reflexive muscle response, not consciousness. The bird is already gone.
5. **Scald.** Dip the carcass in 145-150 degree Fahrenheit water for 30-60 seconds. This loosens the feathers for plucking.
6. **Pluck.** Remove all feathers. A mechanical plucker (drum with rubber fingers) does this in 15-30 seconds. Hand plucking takes several minutes per bird.
7. **Eviscerate.** Open the body cavity carefully (cutting through the abdominal wall without puncturing the intestines — the primary food safety risk). Remove all internal organs intact. Identify and save the edible giblets (heart, liver, gizzard).
8. **Chill.** Submerge the cleaned carcass in ice water immediately. Internal temperature must drop below 40 degrees Fahrenheit within 4 hours to prevent bacterial growth.
9. **Package and store.** Bag in food-safe packaging. Freeze or refrigerate (use within 3-5 days if refrigerated).

**Larger animal slaughter (with supervision):**
Larger animals (goats, sheep, pigs) are typically stunned with a captive bolt or killed with a precisely placed firearm shot. The shot placement is specific: intersect imaginary lines drawn from each eye to the opposite ear. The animal drops instantly. Bleed immediately by severing the carotid arteries. Skin, eviscerate, and chill.

Your first participation in larger animal processing should be under the direct supervision of an experienced person. This is not work to learn by trial and error.

**Food Safety:**
- Keep everything clean: hands, knives, surfaces, water
- Prevent contamination from gut contents during evisceration (the number one food safety risk in home processing)
- Chill carcasses rapidly — bacteria multiply fastest between 40 and 140 degrees Fahrenheit
- Handle raw meat separately from ready-to-eat food
- When in doubt, discard — food safety is not a place to take chances

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Systematic Health Monitoring
**Developing well:** The student checks animals with purpose every day. Records are current. They can identify a change from baseline and explain what it might mean.

**Developing slowly:** Health checks are cursory or skipped. Records are incomplete. The student does not connect observations to potential conditions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Review the student's health records. Do they show daily entries with specific observations, or are they filled in retroactively with generic notes? The records reveal whether the monitoring is real or performative.

### 2. Common Condition Management
**Developing well:** The student has treated at least one condition and can describe the assessment, treatment decision, and outcome. They can distinguish between conditions they can manage and conditions that need a vet.

**Developing slowly:** The student has not treated any conditions (either none have arisen, or they deferred everything to the mentor).

**Diagnostic questions:** If no health issues have arisen naturally, create teaching opportunities: have the student practice FAMACHA scoring on all goats/sheep, practice injection technique on an orange (standard training method), or assess hoof condition and trim as needed.

### 3. Slaughter and Processing Competence
**Developing well:** The student has participated in at least one complete slaughter and processing from start to finish. They can describe the process, executed their role competently, and maintained composure. They ate the food they processed.

**Developing slowly:** The student has not yet participated, or participated but was unable to engage (could not watch, could not participate in processing, left before completion).

**Diagnostic questions:** If the student was unable to engage, this is not a failure — it is an indication that more preparation or more time is needed. Some students need to observe twice before participating. Some need a longer conversation beforehand. Adjust the approach, but do not abandon the goal. If the student eats meat, this experience is a necessary part of their food education.

### 4. Food Safety
**Developing well:** The student handles meat with consistent attention to temperature, cleanliness, and contamination prevention. They can explain why each safety practice matters.

**Developing slowly:** The student is careless with meat handling — leaving carcasses at ambient temperature, not cleaning equipment between animals, or not chilling rapidly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask the student: "What is the temperature danger zone for meat? How fast does the carcass need to reach safe temperature? What happens if gut contents contact the meat?" If they cannot answer, the food safety education needs reinforcement before they process independently.
